Q1: Honestly, my study routine was chaotic at best. I tried to dedicate around 21 hours a week, but with nine online courses running simultaneously, I was constantly jumping between subjects. I'd make these elaborate study schedules on Sunday nights, but by Tuesday they'd fall apart because something urgent would come up. I mostly studied late at night when my anxiety was keeping me awake anyway, reading and re-reading my notes until things started to stick.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and how panicked I was feeling. Some weeks I'd barely touch certain courses, then cram everything in the last few days before deadlines. I wanted to be consistent, I really did, but managing nine courses with limited resources meant I was always putting out fires. The regularity I craved just never materialized no matter how hard I tried to establish it.
Q3: Usually panic prompted me to log in—realizing a deadline was approaching or getting a notification about something I'd missed. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd forgotten anything important, which happened more than I'd like to admit. I also logged in when my peer support group would message me about discussions or study sessions. The anxiety of not knowing what I was missing kept me coming back frequently.
Q4: The LMS did become routine in a stressful way—I'd check it first thing every morning like checking for bad news. My peer support connections helped normalize it because we'd coordinate through the platform. After about a month, logging in became automatic, almost compulsive. But it never felt comfortable, more like a necessary surveillance of my own academic standing.
Q5: The discussion forums were surprisingly helpful because connecting with peers made me feel less alone in struggling. I also found the reading materials most useful since that matches how I learn best—I could download PDFs and annotate them heavily. The videos were good too; I watched over 200 minutes worth because sometimes I needed concepts explained multiple ways before they clicked. Anything I could engage with through reading and writing felt most natural to me.
Q6: I often skipped or delayed the optional practice quizzes because I was so overwhelmed just keeping up with required work. Some of the supplementary readings piled up unread because I had to prioritize what would directly impact my grades. I also minimally used some of the interactive tools because they required stable internet, and my connection at home isn't always reliable with my limited resources.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to assessments or helped me understand something I was genuinely confused about. When peers in my support group recommended something as helpful, I'd prioritize it. I also valued anything that could be done offline since I could work on it during my commute or when my internet was spotty. If something felt like busywork with no clear payoff, I just couldn't justify the time given everything else on my plate.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required work already consumed more time than I had, so optional things triggered guilt rather than curiosity. I'd see them listed and feel this wave of anxiety knowing I should do them but couldn't. Required activities at least had clear consequences, which paradoxically made them easier to prioritize even when overwhelming.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and readings, then work through them on paper with handwritten notes since that's how I retain information best. I used external YouTube videos to supplement concepts I didn't understand. I also borrowed textbooks from the library because I couldn't afford to buy them all, and none of that studying showed up anywhere in the system.
Q10: So many situations. I'd spend hours rewriting my notes by hand, making summary sheets, and quizzing myself with flashcards I made—none of which the LMS could track. When preparing for exams, I'd print everything out and study in the library without any digital trace. My best learning happened in study sessions with my peer support group where we'd work through problems together on whiteboards. The LMS only saw me download a file once, not the five hours I spent with it afterward.
Q11: This was basically my standard approach. I'd access materials once, download everything I could, and then work with them extensively offline. My internet isn't always reliable, and I couldn't afford to depend on it for studying. I'd watch videos once online, take detailed notes, and then review those notes repeatedly without ever rewatching. The LMS would show minimal engagement, but those materials were worn thin from use.
Q12: There were several breaking points, honestly. Around midterms, managing nine courses became nearly impossible, and my anxiety spiked so badly I could barely focus. Financial stress compounded everything—I was worrying about rent while trying to study organic chemistry. When multiple deadlines clustered together, I'd freeze up and accomplish nothing, which only made the anxiety worse. My attendance dropped to almost nothing during those periods because I was drowning.
Q13: Constantly. The cognitive overload from nine courses meant I was always fatigued and confused about priorities. I'd log into the LMS intending to work on one course and get distracted by urgent notifications from another. Competing responsibilities like work and basic survival needs meant the LMS often got accessed in fragmented, unfocused bursts. Sometimes I'd stare at the screen too exhausted to actually process anything.
Q14: More times than I can count. I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through materials, but my mind would be elsewhere—worrying about money, about other deadlines, about whether I was failing. Sometimes I'd watch videos while so tired that nothing registered, but technically I'd 'watched' them. There were nights I'd click through readings just to feel like I was doing something, without actually learning anything.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts and peer interactions even though I knew they helped my learning, because they required mental energy I didn't have. I avoided starting major assignments until the last minute because the scope of them triggered my anxiety. I knew this pattern was self-destructive, but the overwhelm made it feel impossible to begin things early. It's like my brain would shut down when facing something big, so I'd do smaller, less important tasks instead.
Q16: Deadlines completely dictated my LMS usage. I'd have bursts of intense activity in the 48 hours before something was due, then relative quiet until the next crisis. My login frequency was high partly because I was constantly checking what was due when. The deadline pressure helped me focus but also meant I rarely engaged deeply with material—just enough to complete what was required. It wasn't learning; it was surviving.
Q17: Feedback was actually really motivating when I got it. Seeing that I scored well on assignments despite my chaotic process gave me hope. The peer support intervention helped because getting encouragement from classmates made me feel less like a failure. But feedback often came too late to change my approach for that particular course, so its impact was more emotional than practical.
Q18: The course structure was overwhelming with so much content released at once. I would have benefited from more guided pacing, but instead I had to self-regulate across nine courses simultaneously. The flexibility that was supposed to help actually hurt because I had too many choices about what to prioritize. When modules were clearly sequenced with obvious importance markers, I engaged more consistently.
Q19: Having materials available for download made engagement much easier given my situation. The discussion forums being active helped because peer interaction kept me accountable. However, the sheer volume of content across all my courses made engagement harder—I never felt caught up. Also, some resources required consistent internet access, which was a barrier for me. Clearer prioritization of essential versus supplementary content would have helped enormously.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat. High activity can mean someone is desperately trying to figure out what's important, searching through materials anxiously without producing output. It could also mean they're engaging deeply with learning but struggling to translate that into formal submissions. For me, my low assignment completion despite high engagement reflected being spread too thin—I was learning but couldn't always demonstrate it formally across all nine courses.
Q21: This also partially reflects my experience. My exam score was decent despite terrible attendance and low task completion because I studied intensively offline in ways the LMS couldn't see. Some students just learn differently—they might read materials once carefully rather than repeatedly accessing them. The LMS measures clicks, not comprehension. I think this pattern suggests the student found efficient ways to learn that don't require constant platform interaction.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns of access, timing relative to deadlines, and participation in trackable activities like discussions. They're good at identifying who's completely absent. But they miss the quality of engagement—whether someone is actually learning or just clicking through. They can't see offline study, emotional state, or the depth of processing happening. They also can't distinguish between focused engagement and anxious, scattered browsing.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious and thinking about concepts even outside of study time. True engagement involves connecting ideas together and wanting to discuss them with others. For me, it also means feeling like I belong in the course despite my struggles. It's mental and emotional investment, not just time logged on a platform.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much I was learning during my offline study sessions or how valuable my peer support group was for keeping me going. It doesn't capture my anxiety levels or how financial stress affected my engagement patterns. It can't see that my high login frequency was often driven by panic rather than productive engagement. Most importantly, it can't show that despite appearing to barely complete anything, I was working incredibly hard and actually learning—I just couldn't keep up with the formal requirements across nine courses with limited resources.
